title: Tehran

jokes.text: You don't get a get-well card when you have plastic surgery.
jokes.setup: Amy is on the phone with her mother, discussing someone losing their job and being 'radioactive for a while,' then pivoting to a card.
jokes.punchline: The reason no card was sent is revealed: the recipient had plastic surgery, not a real illness — undercutting the sympathy framing entirely.
jokes.explanation: Sharp, economical rule-of-the-world observation delivered as a throwaway. The logic is airtight and funny — a social norm articulated crisply. Solid Veep opener energy.

jokes.text: I've extended my stay in Israel for another day. So I may need to borrow some toothpaste.
jokes.setup: Amy mentions a geopolitically significant extension of a Middle East peace tour.
jokes.punchline: The immediate practical concern she raises is borrowing toothpaste — a comically mundane deflation of the grand diplomatic moment.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep move: undercut the epic with the absurdly mundane. The juxtaposition of 'geopolitical extension' and 'toothpaste' is crisp and well-timed.

jokes.text: See that tour? I set that up. See that shelf? I made that.
jokes.setup: News coverage praises President Meyer's successful Middle East peace tour.
jokes.punchline: Ben compares his geopolitical contribution (setting up the tour) to making a shelf — collapsing grand achievement and domestic craft into the same self-congratulatory breath.
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly economical. Ben's weary, self-satisfied cynicism is perfectly captured by the parallelism — tour and shelf given equal weight. 'See that shelf? I made that.' is immediately quotable and deeply character-specific. The rhetorical structure (anaphora) does real comedic work.

jokes.text: Where's the woman who changed world politics? — She's taking a piss. / She's freshening up.
jokes.setup: Someone asks admiringly where the transformative world leader is.
jokes.punchline: Two staffers immediately answer with the mundane biological reality ('taking a piss') and then the PR-sanitized version ('freshening up') in quick succession.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured two-beat joke. The first answer punctures the mythologizing; the second shows the spin reflex is so automatic that even Mike can't help softening it a half-second later. The gap between those two responses is a whole commentary on political image-making.

jokes.text: I'm so tired, I could sleep a horse. Or whatever that word thing is.
jokes.setup: Selina is clearly exhausted and tries to use the idiom 'sleep like a log' or similar.
jokes.punchline: She mangles the idiom entirely ('sleep a horse') and then consciously acknowledges she's butchered it without knowing what the correct version is.
jokes.explanation: Selina's exhausted word-scrambling is a well-established character trait in Veep — she's too tired and too vain to admit ignorance, so she patches the damage by dismissing it. 'Or whatever that word thing is' is the real punchline.

jokes.text: Wait, Ben, is this Ambien? How many of these have you taken? — Look, I need to sleep. I got all jacked up on licorice last night and I was belly dancing till dawn.
jokes.setup: Mike notices Ben is taking pills and questions what they are.
jokes.punchline: Ben's explanation — that he needed Ambien because he was jacked up on licorice and belly dancing until dawn — is so specific and absurd it becomes its own comedic event.
jokes.explanation: The specificity is everything here. 'Licorice' and 'belly dancing till dawn' are so hyper-specific and incongruous that the image is genuinely hilarious. Ben's deadpan delivery of this absurdist self-portrait against the backdrop of a historic diplomatic mission is textbook Veep: the personal catastrophe eclipsing the geopolitical. Near the 8 anchor level.

jokes.text: Imagine being detained for two weeks just 'cause you're a journalist and a shithead. — Yeah.
jokes.setup: Mike frames Leon West's detention sympathetically as a journalist.
jokes.punchline: He undercuts the sympathy entirely by adding 'and a shithead' — and Ben's simple 'Yeah' confirms both descriptors are equally factual.
jokes.explanation: Maximum compression. The pivot from civil liberties concern to personal disdain in three words ('and a shithead') is perfectly Veep. Ben's monosyllabic agreement does the heavy lifting on the punchline.

jokes.text: It's a good job we had nothing to do with his being detained. — Yeah, wouldn't that be terrible? — Mm-hmm. — Wait, did we have something to do with...? [music plays]
jokes.setup: Mike and Ben are performing careful plausible-deniability language about Leon's detention when a third person starts to catch on.
jokes.punchline: The question is cut off by triumphant music — the show literally drowns out the incriminating realization with celebratory fanfare.
jokes.explanation: Structurally elegant. The comedic escalation of carefully worded denial followed by dawning realization, cut off by ironic triumphalist music, is a sophisticated three-part joke. The music as comedic punchline is a visual/audio gag that earns its surprise. This is Veep firing on all cylinders — dark, structurally inventive, and narratively loaded.

jokes.text: Hey, you know what you are? — What? — You're an amazing man. — Thank you. — And you know what amazing men get? — What? — Two-day weekend. — This weekend? — Yeah. — I'm going to the cottage with Wendy!
jokes.setup: Selina builds Gary up with an elaborate compliment structure that sounds like it's leading to a gift or reward.
jokes.punchline: The 'reward' is a two-day weekend — a basic employment right delivered as a grand prize. Gary's ecstatic response ('I'm going to the cottage with Wendy!') amplifies how low the bar is for his happiness.
jokes.explanation: The slow-burn structure here is Selina at her most manipulatively endearing. The increasingly earnest buildup to 'two-day weekend' as if it were a gold watch, and Gary's total sincerity in receiving it, says everything about their relationship dynamic with maximum efficiency.

jokes.text: Oh, God. I have to wait here another hour before we can fly out? ... Let's use the hour, then, okay? Let's cure diabetes or learn Italian.
jokes.setup: Selina is told she must wait an extra hour in Iran.
jokes.punchline: Rather than accept idle time gracefully, she proposes either curing diabetes or learning Italian as equally viable hour-long activities.
jokes.explanation: Selina's manic energy finding expression in absurd presidential ambition ('cure diabetes') next to trivial tourist aspiration ('learn Italian') in the same breath is perfectly calibrated character comedy. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I gotta run around like a Labrador or a guy on fire or something like that.
jokes.setup: Selina explains why she can't take a nap — she has too much energy.
jokes.punchline: The similes she reaches for are a hyperactive dog and a man on fire — and she offers them as equivalent options, then trails off with 'or something like that.'
jokes.explanation: More Selina word-salad, but this one has a genuine surreal quality. 'A guy on fire' as a simile for energetic happiness is weirdly specific and funny. The trailing 'or something like that' is her characteristic self-correction that never actually corrects anything.

jokes.text: How are the folks on the press plane? — Sober. — They got the shakes so bad, I think I see the plane rattling.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about the press traveling with her.
jokes.punchline: The press corps are so alcoholic that their collective withdrawal shakes are visibly rattling the plane.
jokes.explanation: Efficient two-beat joke. 'Sober' is the first punchline (the worst possible answer given the dry country context), and then the visual of the rattling plane escalates it perfectly. Good structural escalation.

jokes.text: An hour? These guys could be drinking jet fuel.
jokes.setup: Mike is told the press will have to wait another hour in a dry country (Iran).
jokes.punchline: The press corps's alcoholism is so severe that Mike genuinely fears they'll find industrial alternatives.
jokes.explanation: Callback to the plane-rattling joke, escalating the press alcoholism gag. 'Drinking jet fuel' is a perfectly absurd escalation. Tight.

jokes.text: I could, like, bang a nail into that wall with my gaze. Do you know what I mean?
jokes.setup: Selina refuses a shoulder massage and insists she's sharply focused.
jokes.punchline: Her description of her focus — being able to bang a nail with her gaze — is simultaneously deranged and vivid. The 'Do you know what I mean?' asks for validation on an objectively insane statement.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Bang a nail into that wall with my gaze' is a genuinely original expression of manic focus — specific enough to be vivid, strange enough to be funny, and perfectly Selina in its grandiosity. The 'Do you know what I mean?' is the topper — she's asking for social confirmation of something unconfirmable. Easily comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: What kind of wood is this table? — I don't know. Let me check. — I think it's ash. — Uh-uh. — I'm gonna redo my kitchen.
jokes.setup: Selina is supposed to be handling a diplomatic crisis but instead starts examining the furniture.
jokes.punchline: The scene culminates in Selina deciding to redo her kitchen based on an Iranian meeting room table — a total displacement of presidential priorities.
jokes.explanation: The joke is that Selina — on Ambien and adrenaline, in historic Iran — is making interior design decisions. The dissociation from her context is the comedy. 'I'm gonna redo my kitchen' as a presidential resolution in Tehran is perfect.

jokes.text: How do you respond to Senator O'Brien's comment that the American people need a president, not a Meyertollah?
jokes.setup: A reporter asks the VP about a political attack on President Meyer.
jokes.punchline: 'Meyertollah' — a portmanteau of Meyer and Ayatollah — lands as both a clever political pun and a genuinely cutting attack given the Iran trip context.
jokes.explanation: 'Meyertollah' is genuinely excellent political wordplay — it's the kind of pun that's actually smart, contextually specific, and cutting in a real-world political way. It would earn genuine laughs in a press room and among viewers simultaneously. High quotability.

jokes.text: It's a cheap pun, so absolutely expect... — That finishing sentences thing is... — Irritating? Very. — But sometimes necessary when people are being slow or dull.
jokes.setup: Doyle dismisses 'Meyertollah' as a cheap pun and starts to finish the press secretary's sentences.
jokes.punchline: The Doyle character's habit of cutting people off is called out, he finishes the sentence calling it out, and then defends it — a meta-loop that's both character-specific and self-aware.
jokes.explanation: Clever structural joke: Doyle cuts off the person criticizing him for cutting people off, and then justifies it. The recursive quality is genuinely sharp. Strong character integration — this IS Doyle.

jokes.text: When's Mike back? — Hilarious. Well done. — No people skills. Like a robot.
jokes.setup: During a serious press briefing, a reporter asks the non-sequitur question 'When's Mike back?'
jokes.punchline: Doyle sarcastically praises the question, then the aide dismisses the reporter as robotic — the irony being Doyle himself just complained about people being slow.
jokes.explanation: Competent setup for Doyle's snide character, but the joke's impact is diluted because the reporter's non sequitur isn't really established enough to earn the 'Hilarious, well done' response. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Turns out the Iranians need everything in triplicate. Maybe that's 'cause they invented numbers, huh?
jokes.setup: Mike apologizes to the press corps for the delay, citing Iranian bureaucratic requirements.
jokes.punchline: He then notes the Iranians invented numbers as if that explains the bureaucratic love of triplicate — conflating ancient mathematical achievement with modern administrative frustration.
jokes.explanation: A slightly risky observational joke that walks the line of cultural commentary. The logic — they invented numbers so they love using them — is silly enough to read as Mike's stupidity rather than genuine xenophobia. Moderately effective.

jokes.text: Let's face it, he's probably gonna get a book deal out of this.
jokes.setup: The team discusses Leon West's imminent release after two weeks of detention.
jokes.punchline: Mike's first thought is Leon's commercial upside — 'book deal' — revealing both cynicism about media culture and total lack of empathy.
jokes.explanation: Decent throwaway. Observationally true about journalists in crisis situations, but not distinctly Mike — any cynical character could say this. Competent filler.

jokes.text: See that? Catlike reflexes. [catches thrown bottle]
jokes.setup: Mike tells the press corps not to start drinking yet, out of courtesy, and presumably something is thrown to him.
jokes.punchline: Mike catches whatever is thrown and immediately self-congratulates on his 'catlike reflexes' — the sort of small vanity that's unexpectedly charming.
jokes.explanation: Visual gag inferred from context. Mike's self-satisfaction is the joke, and 'catlike reflexes' is a funny bit of personal inflation. Lower craft because it's a fairly standard physical comedy beat.

jokes.text: In my family, basketball is a religion. Also Catholicism. That's actually the main one.
jokes.setup: Doyle meets NBA star Freddy Wallace and establishes his family's passion for basketball as religious in intensity.
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies that actual Catholicism is also a religion in his family — and in fact the main one — undercutting the basketball metaphor.
jokes.explanation: Near-perfect joke structure. The setup is a common sports-as-religion cliché, and then the two-beat correction ('Also Catholicism. That's actually the main one.') is a perfectly timed reversal that also tells you everything about Doyle's literal-minded, politically-Catholic personality. Economy is excellent. This is comparable to the 8.0 anchor example.

jokes.text: L is for ladies who play tennis, G is for guys who do other guys, B is for bisexual — I couldn't think of one to go for that — and T... — Is for tucking it in or tacking it on.
jokes.setup: A staffer tells Jonah to remember the LGBT acronym for the Rainbow Jersey event.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's mnemonic for LGBT is breathtakingly ignorant and reductive in a way that perfectly captures his character — and the aide's 'tucking it in or tacking it on' for T is both a punchline and a correction that's somehow equally offensive.
jokes.explanation: Strong character comedy — Jonah's mnemonics perfectly reveal his blunt, unreflective worldview, and the aide's correction ('tucking it in or tacking it on') is both funnier and equally incorrect, making the punchline land twice. The B admission ('I couldn't think of one') is a great mid-joke beat.

jokes.text: It's transgender, actually. — Thanks for killing my joke and then mutilating it.
jokes.setup: Gary correctly identifies 'T' as transgender after Jonah and the aide's wrong answers.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's response — 'Thanks for killing my joke and then mutilating it' — is a genuinely funny complaint about a correction that was always going to land awkwardly given the subject matter.
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'killing my joke and then mutilating it' is a brilliant complaint structure — the double verb ('killing' and 'mutilating') does unexpected work. It's also inadvertently dark given the T context. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Well, as my grandfather never said, let's go be inclusive.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries to rally the team with an inspirational-sounding send-off before the Rainbow Jersey event.
jokes.punchline: He prefaces it with 'as my grandfather never said' — implying his grandfather was notably non-inclusive — as a casual confession wrapped in a quasi-inspirational structure.
jokes.explanation: 'As my grandfather never said' is a clever inversion of the inspirational-quote formula. The implication is darkly funny — Jonah casually revealing his family history of bigotry as a throwaway qualifier. Tight and quotable.

jokes.text: I could not be more likable if I had given both of my kidneys to some sick kid.
jokes.setup: Selina is reviewing good polling numbers and describing her likability to her team.
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of 'both' kidneys (sacrificing her own health to appear selfless) and the casual 'some sick kid' (dehumanizing the recipient) reveals Selina's likability is entirely performative and self-referential.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Both kidneys' implies she'd die in the service of appearing generous, and 'some sick kid' is perfectly callous — the child is just a prop in Selina's self-image. This couldn't be said by anyone other than Selina Meyer. Exceptional character integration. Quotability is among the episode's highest.

jokes.text: Gary, I want some champagne. — Ma'am, we're in a tea totalitarian state.
jokes.setup: Selina asks for champagne in Iran.
jokes.punchline: Gary says they're in a 'tea totalitarian state' — a pun fusing 'teetotal' (abstaining from alcohol) with 'totalitarian' (the political system), perfectly accurate on both levels.
jokes.explanation: 'Tea totalitarian state' is exceptional wordplay — it works on multiple levels simultaneously (alcohol ban, political system, Iran-specific context), is maximally compressed (three words of work), and Gary delivering it deadpan makes it land harder. Among the best individual jokes in the episode. Comparable to above the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, the vice president... [instead of 'president'] on my team.
jokes.setup: NBA star Freddy Wallace gives a speech supporting the event, presumably written for President Meyer's appearance.
jokes.punchline: He catches himself mid-sentence and corrects 'president' to 'vice president' — a devastating public reminder of Doyle's secondary status — and then the phrase 'on my team' resonates awkwardly given the homophobia-in-sports context.
jokes.explanation: A two-level joke: the president/vice president correction publicly diminishes Doyle, and 'on my team' has an unintended double meaning in the LGBTQ sports context. The scene transitions suggest Doyle sitting with this humiliation, which is a strong reaction beat.

jokes.text: I put the champagne in the water bottles. — You don't have to explain. I can tell what you did.
jokes.setup: Gary presents Selina with what appear to be water bottles.
jokes.punchline: Gary explains the champagne deception, and Selina cuts him off — she already figured it out, and his explaining defeats the whole point of the covert operation.
jokes.explanation: Short and functional. Selina's exasperated 'you don't have to explain' works because Gary's earnest helpfulness consistently undermines what he's trying to achieve. Good but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, look at Doyle. — Did he have his teeth capped? — Oh, my God. The last surviving Golden Girl.
jokes.setup: Selina and an aide watch Vice President Doyle on TV at his Rainbow Jersey appearance.
jokes.punchline: Selina describes Doyle as 'the last surviving Golden Girl' — a sharp, aged-specific insult that also diminishes him by comparison to a group of elderly women.
jokes.explanation: 'The last surviving Golden Girl' is a great Selina insult — specific pop-cultural, age-targeting, and dismissive simultaneously. The teeth-capping setup makes it earned. Very quotable.

jokes.text: When I was a... boy, it was my gay friends who taught me how to be tolerant and how to be true to yourself and how to dance to Madonna.
jokes.setup: Doyle is giving a sincere-seeming speech about LGBT inclusion and personal growth.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of things his gay friends taught him culminates in 'how to dance to Madonna' — a specific, stereotyped detail that derails the earnest moment.
jokes.explanation: The speech-as-disaster is a Veep staple, and this one works because the escalation from 'tolerant' to 'true to yourself' to 'dance to Madonna' has a perfect rhythm — the pause before 'Madonna' is the comedian's beat. Also the 'When I was a... boy' hesitation suggests a more embarrassing truth avoided.

jokes.text: Madonna? — What? — Was I supposed to say that?
jokes.setup: Doyle has just said 'dance to Madonna' in his LGBT speech.
jokes.punchline: An aide's horrified 'Madonna?' and Doyle's confused 'Was I supposed to say that?' — genuinely unsure if the pop star reference was inappropriate or just off-script.
jokes.explanation: The reaction beat extends and deepens the joke — Doyle genuinely doesn't know if Madonna was the right or wrong call, which is the whole point about his disconnect from the community he's speaking to.

jokes.text: You know, back in college, my... girlfriends would... Boyfriends... my friends...
jokes.setup: Doyle tries to recover from 'Madonna' with a personal anecdote about his college friends.
jokes.punchline: He corrects himself mid-word — 'girlfriends' to 'Boyfriends' to 'my friends' — each correction making things progressively more fraught and confusing at an LGBT event.
jokes.explanation: The mid-speech correction cascade is a masterclass in cringe comedy. Each correction creates a worse problem than it solves — 'Boyfriends' at an LGBT event from a straight guy implying things he doesn't mean — and the final deflation to 'my friends' is a surrender that's funnier than either correction.

jokes.text: Perhaps it did need some reworking. [beat after Doyle's speech disaster]
jokes.setup: Someone points out the speech may have needed revision after Doyle's 'Madonna' and girlfriend/boyfriend catastrophe.
jokes.punchline: 'Perhaps it did need some reworking' is pure British understatement in response to a spectacular public disaster.
jokes.explanation: Classic Iannucci understatement — an entire catastrophe dismissed in six words. The dryness is the joke. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: He is the asshole of an asshole's asshole. I feel bad for the guys who had to guard him.
jokes.setup: Selina is asked if Leon West is a friend.
jokes.punchline: Her description of Leon reaches fractal levels of contempt — 'asshole of an asshole's asshole' — and then pivots to feeling sorry for his Iranian guards, extending the insult into absurdist compassion.
jokes.explanation: 'The asshole of an asshole's asshole' is genuinely brilliant as an insult construction — the recursive self-reference creates a fractal of contempt. But the real punchline is pivoting to sympathy for his guards, which extends and deepens the joke. Among the episode's best lines.

jokes.text: A face I never thought I'd want to kiss. — I still don't.
jokes.setup: Mike greets Leon with what sounds like it's building toward warmth.
jokes.punchline: 'I still don't' — the apparent warmth is immediately retracted. The setup teases a heartfelt reunion and the punchline confirms the opposite.
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-beat joke. Maximum economy — the setup creates genuine ambiguity about whether Mike will express real relief, and 'I still don't' detonates it instantly. Among the most structurally efficient jokes in the episode. Easily at the 8.0 anchor level.

jokes.text: Nice hotel? — Yeah, I'll have something to say about it on TripAdvisor. Worst minibar ever.
jokes.setup: Mike asks Leon about his accommodations during Iranian detention.
jokes.punchline: Leon frames his captivity through the lens of a hotel review — TripAdvisor, minibar — as if it were simply an unsatisfactory business trip.
jokes.explanation: Leon's reporter bravado meeting consumer complaint culture is a sharp satirical mash-up. 'Worst minibar ever' as the capstone complaint from a man just released from political detention is excellent dark comedy.

jokes.text: And you, sir, get to fly home on Air Force One. — Whoa. — Yeah. — I'm shallow enough for that to be exciting.
jokes.setup: Mike offers Leon the perk of flying home on Air Force One after his ordeal.
jokes.punchline: Leon's self-aware confession — 'I'm shallow enough for that to be exciting' — is funny because it's honest, and the implicit critique is that being flown on Air Force One should feel like a sufficient bribe after being deliberately detained.
jokes.explanation: Sharp self-aware line for Leon that doubles as character establishment. His honesty about being seduced by the presidential perk is funny and earns him audience sympathy while also revealing the bribery at work.

jokes.text: Did you know it can survive a nuclear blast? — Yeah. — Not that you guys are planning one. I... ignore me. I don't know why I said that. — His English isn't very good.
jokes.setup: Mike enthusiastically tells Leon about Air Force One's nuclear-blast survivability while Iranian liaison Abbas is present.
jokes.punchline: Mike realizes mid-sentence that saying 'not that you guys are planning one' to an Iranian is spectacularly awkward, then tries to walk it back, and then implausibly invokes 'his English isn't very good' about someone who demonstrably speaks perfect English.
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly escalating cringe sequence. The nuclear comment is a gift that keeps giving — Mike's attempted recovery ('ignore me, I don't know why I said that') makes it worse, and the 'his English isn't very good' callback, which has already been contradicted by Abbas himself, is the perfect topper.

jokes.text: The president has a big-ass scotch on the rocks for you. — Yeah. — Which is iced tea. It's iced tea.
jokes.setup: Mike tells Leon about the welcome drink waiting for him on the plane.
jokes.punchline: After building up 'big-ass scotch on the rocks,' Mike immediately corrects himself — they're in a dry country, so it's iced tea. The deflation is total.
jokes.explanation: Solid but not exceptional. The setup-deflation structure is reliable Veep humor, and the repetition of 'It's iced tea' lands a bit of emphasis. Good functional comedy.

jokes.text: So let's celebrate all things L and GBT.
jokes.setup: Doyle gives what should be an inclusivity statement.
jokes.punchline: He says 'L and GBT' — grouping the first letter separately, suggesting he's learned the letters but not the meaning or cadence of the acronym.
jokes.explanation: Doyle's wrong parsing of 'L and GBT' signals he's memorized the letters but not the word — a perfect continuing thread of the whole event's catastrophic relationship with the alphabet.

jokes.text: Though these days we use the more inclusive LGBTQ. — We do! — Um, and I... respect all people's rights to be a Q. — Or rather just Q.
jokes.setup: Freddy Wallace corrects Doyle mid-event by upgrading the acronym to LGBTQ.
jokes.punchline: Doyle's attempt to incorporate 'Q' produces 'be a Q' — as if Q were a club membership — and his self-correction to 'just Q' makes it somehow worse.
jokes.explanation: Escalation comedy working perfectly. Doyle's 'right to be a Q' is genuinely hilarious — treating Q like a certificate you earn — and the self-correction ('or rather just Q') shows him trying and making it worse. Each iteration of this running gag tops the previous one.

jokes.text: Sounds like he's learning a sex alphabet.
jokes.setup: Doyle is fumbling through LGBTQ terminology live on stage.
jokes.punchline: An observer's commentary: 'Sounds like he's learning a sex alphabet' — a pitch-perfect description of Doyle's stilted, letter-by-letter approach.
jokes.explanation: 'Learning a sex alphabet' is an excellently compressed observation — it captures precisely what Doyle is doing (treating each letter as a separate unfamiliar entity) with a perfectly absurd framing. Very quotable.

jokes.text: I've seen a salmon in a grizzly's mouth look less panicky than that.
jokes.setup: Amy watches Doyle's painful performance at the Rainbow Jersey event.
jokes.punchline: The simile — salmon in a grizzly's mouth — is vivid, specific, and perfectly calibrated to describe visible panic being consumed alive.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. Amy's nature-documentary similes are a character signature, and 'salmon in a grizzly's mouth' is genuinely poetic in its specificity. The image is immediately vivid and the comparative ('look LESS panicky') is the perfect construction. Highly quotable, clearly above the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: Ironically, the straight guy is very stiff around the gay guy. — Our polling said that he was widely perceived as quite wooden. — Why were you polling him?
jokes.setup: The team observes Doyle's visible discomfort with Freddy Wallace.
jokes.punchline: Kent reveals polling exists on Doyle's 'woodenness,' and Amy's 'Why were you polling him?' exposes Kent's covert veep replacement polling, which then becomes its own crisis.
jokes.explanation: A great three-beat joke with structural elegance — the irony observation (stiff/wooden puns), the polling reveal (which is funnier for being framed as a 'wooden' perception), and Amy's devastating 'why were you polling him?' which opens a new plot crisis. The wordplay on 'stiff' and 'wooden' in an LGBT context adds another layer.

jokes.text: Yet again, the left hand has no idea what the right hand is doing and the freakish middle hand is punching me repeatedly in the tits.
jokes.setup: Amy learns that Doyle was promised he wouldn't be polled against other VP candidates — a promise no one told her about.
jokes.punchline: The standard 'left hand/right hand' idiom is extended with a surreal third element — 'the freakish middle hand is punching me repeatedly in the tits' — that demolishes the metaphor with physical specificity.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The setup (standard idiom) is used as infrastructure to launch something genuinely unexpected. 'The freakish middle hand' is a perfect surreal invention, and 'punching me repeatedly in the tits' is specific, physical, and feminine-coded in a way that deepens the character. The word 'repeatedly' is doing crucial work — it's not a one-time assault but an ongoing state. This is in the same tier as 'Snip, snap' in structural audacity, if not quite as culturally embedded.

jokes.text: Well, that was an education. — I mean, what I really want to do is get into lobbying. But it's only been, like, six weeks and three days, so I'm not sweating it.
jokes.setup: Dan reflects on the Rainbow Jersey event with a vague comment, then pivots to his own post-firing situation.
jokes.punchline: He's tracking his unemployment to the day ('six weeks and three days') while claiming not to be sweating it — the precision betraying the nonchalance.
jokes.explanation: Classic Dan Egan self-revelation through contradiction. The 'six weeks and three days' is the tell — Dan absolutely is sweating it, and the specificity of 'three days' confirms he's counting every day obsessively.

jokes.text: I can make you so rich, you can pay Bill Gates to give you a lap dance.
jokes.setup: Sidney Purcell pitches the lobby firm's financial promise to Dan.
jokes.punchline: The specific, absurd metric of wealth — affording Bill Gates's lap dance — is both a measure of prosperity and a deeply degrading image for Gates.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely excellent line. The wealth metric of 'paying Bill Gates for a lap dance' is so specific and absurd that it achieves a kind of comic perfection. It simultaneously measures incredible wealth, implies dominance over the world's richest man, and conjures a profoundly funny image. The specificity of 'Bill Gates' rather than a generic rich person is crucial.

jokes.text: Can you take on clients who are best served by you shitting on the Meyer agenda? — Oh, well, let me think about that. — Yeah. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Sidney asks Dan if he can lobby against his former boss's agenda.
jokes.punchline: Dan pauses as if genuinely weighing a moral question, then immediately agrees — the pause being entirely performative.
jokes.explanation: Good character beat for Dan — his 'moral consideration' is entirely theatrical. Works as character comedy but not especially quotable or original.

jokes.text: We'll get to that doorstep thing later, though. — Yeah, I'll still do that. I'm into it.
jokes.setup: Sidney had earlier mentioned 'taking a dump on Mike McLintock's doorstep' as metaphor, then clarified it was metaphorical.
jokes.punchline: Dan volunteers to literally do the doorstep thing anyway — revealing both his contempt for Mike and his enthusiasm for the transgression.
jokes.explanation: Dan's eagerness to perform the literal version of a metaphorical instruction is perfectly character-specific. 'I'm into it' as an enthusiastic volunteer is a great button.

jokes.text: He needs deodorant. — Uh-huh.
jokes.setup: Leon West has just boarded Air Force One after two weeks of detention.
jokes.punchline: Gary's first observation about the freed journalist is that he needs deodorant — the most banal, pragmatic reaction to a historic moment.
jokes.explanation: A very short, functional joke that punctures the ceremonial moment with hygiene concerns. Economical but not particularly original — though the visual implication of Gary's reaction face makes it funnier in performance.

jokes.text: Well, I am so happy to see you, Leon. — We really did work tirelessly for your release. — Well, thank you, Madam President. I haven't slept very much. — Well, I'm sure food is the last thing on your mind. — I know it's the last thing on my mind.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to be presidential and caring while Leon expresses exhaustion.
jokes.punchline: Leon says he hasn't slept; Selina responds 'food is the last thing on your mind' — completely wrong; then confirms it's the last thing on HER mind, revealing the whole conversation is about Selina.
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Selina characterization. She mishears/misinterprets Leon's need entirely, then immediately maps it onto herself. 'I know it's the last thing on my mind' is the perfect pivot — the 'meeting' is instantly exposed as being entirely about Selina's experience. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: Did you just say you do want something to eat? — Yes, thank you. — You do? — Oh, is that food? — No. It's a... It's deodorant.
jokes.setup: Selina is confused about whether Leon wants food; someone produces something and Leon thinks it might be food.
jokes.punchline: It's deodorant — Gary's practical concern from earlier materializing as the thing offered to the hungry released prisoner.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Gary's deodorant observation pays off visually — it's the thing he's been holding, and the starving journalist being offered deodorant instead of food is both absurd and cringe-worthy. The 'You do?' from Selina also shows her still not tracking Leon's needs.

jokes.text: What would you like to eat? — Anything that's not chickpeas.
jokes.setup: Selina finally offers Leon food after the deodorant mix-up.
jokes.punchline: Leon's only specification is 'anything that's not chickpeas' — implying two weeks of Iranian captivity meant two weeks of nothing but chickpeas.
jokes.explanation: 'Anything that's not chickpeas' is a perfectly compressed summary of captivity that generates immediate sympathy AND a laugh. The specificity (not 'Middle Eastern food' but 'chickpeas') is excellent. Great economy.

jokes.text: Hey! Why are those lovely long legs walking away from me? — Probably means you.
jokes.setup: Sidney calls out to someone walking away from him.
jokes.punchline: Dan clarifies 'probably means you' — confirming Sidney's pickup-line energy is directed at Dan himself, which is both absurd and revealing about Sidney's dominant personality.
jokes.explanation: Functional character-establishment joke for Sidney. The 'lovely long legs' direction toward Dan is funny, and Dan's matter-of-fact interpretation is a good reaction beat.

jokes.text: What the fuck? Did you just throw Kent into the blender to save your own ass? — Not fully into the blender. Maybe just a little bit. Just the toe, 'cause...
jokes.setup: Amy confronts Jonah about whether he threw Kent under the bus to deflect blame.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's defense — 'not fully into the blender, maybe just a little bit, just the toe' — takes the blender metaphor and extends it with absurd specificity (a toe) while desperately backpedaling.
jokes.explanation: The 'into the blender' metaphor is good, but Jonah's 'just the toe' extension is excellent — it makes the abstract political betrayal suddenly grotesque and physical, and the toe specificity is a perfect Jonah hedge. 'Just the toe, 'cause...' trailing off is the right button.

jokes.text: That is the Jonah I've been trying to wake up since the day you got here. — And Kent Davison is a disloyal fucking prick. I fucking hate Kent. I want to wipe that neutral expression off his face.
jokes.setup: Amy realizes Jonah's betrayal of Kent was actually useful and tries to encourage more of it.
jokes.punchline: Amy's passionate hatred of Kent ('I want to wipe that neutral expression off his face') is funny because 'neutral expression' is such a specific and petty thing to hate about someone.
jokes.explanation: 'I want to wipe that neutral expression off his face' is a perfectly observed and character-specific insult — hating someone for being expressionless is both absurd and recognizable. Amy's bottled rage finding this very specific outlet is great.

jokes.text: Oh, I can ride her hard, hang her up wet.
jokes.setup: Amy asks how hard Jonah can work his media contact Liz Kerrigan.
jokes.punchline: Jonah uses the horse-riding idiom ('ride her hard and put her away wet') in a context that makes it sound far more sexual than intended — and his total obliviousness to this is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Double-entendre territory, but what makes it work is Jonah's complete unselfawareness. The slightly modified version ('hang her up wet') adds its own strangeness. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: It's just been such a magical time. And I wish I could have visited every country in the whole world. You know? Even New Zealand.
jokes.setup: Selina is summarizing her historic Middle East tour for Leon.
jokes.punchline: 'Even New Zealand' — the addition of New Zealand to her list of desired diplomatic destinations deflates the entire grand tour narrative. New Zealand is so non-threatening and remote that including it as a 'even' speaks to both the tour's self-congratulation and Selina's scattered brain.
jokes.explanation: A small but well-executed Selina line. 'Even New Zealand' as the aspirational capstone of a world peace tour is perfectly bathetic. The 'even' is key — treating a peaceful diplomatic backwater as the bold frontier.

jokes.text: People are now comparing me to Nixon, I heard, right? — They didn't mean your looks.
jokes.setup: Selina excitedly shares that she's being compared to Nixon.
jokes.punchline: Mike's 'They didn't mean your looks' is a casual insult suggesting Nixon comparisons are about politics, not appearance — but also delivering a Nixon-looks dig as a bonus.
jokes.explanation: Crisp two-beat joke. 'They didn't mean your looks' is an economical line that works on multiple levels — clarifying the Nixon comparison, insulting Nixon's looks by implication, and also subtly suggesting Selina looks better than Nixon. Competent political wit.

jokes.text: Just to clarify, you were delayed by a day. — Yeah. — My release was delayed by a day. — Mm-hmm. Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Selina is enthusiastically describing her delayed arrival in Iran to Leon.
jokes.punchline: Leon draws the explicit parallel — Selina was delayed by a day; his release was delayed by a day — and Selina's 'Mm-hmm, Mm-hmm' response is the most cringe-perfect possible non-denial denial.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional cringe comedy. The slow, inevitable logic of Leon connecting the two delays while Selina hums 'Mm-hmm, Mm-hmm' — the President of the United States quietly confirming her own culpability through non-verbal acknowledgment — is masterful. The structure is near-perfect: the setup (Selina bragging), the dawning realization (Leon's logical inference), and the non-verbal confession. Among the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Ben, wake up. — Gary, why are you giving me all that Ambien?
jokes.setup: In the middle of a crisis, Ben has apparently fallen asleep.
jokes.punchline: Ben wakes up confused and blames Gary for the Ambien — a callback to the earlier Ambien revelation and a perfect encapsulation of Ben's exhausted dysfunctionality.
jokes.explanation: The callback works well — Ben actually fell asleep mid-crisis, and waking up still upset about the Ambien he voluntarily took is perfectly absurd. Good payoff for the earlier setup.

jokes.text: Christ, it's that data fuck. — No. I don't know. Yes. Let's just go with I don't know.
jokes.setup: Selina receives bad news about a Liz Kerrigan story on Doyle's dissatisfaction and needs to decide how to respond.
jokes.punchline: Her chain of decision-making — 'No. I don't know. Yes. Let's just go with I don't know' — is a perfect presidential implosion: cycling through all possible positions and settling on maximum non-commitment.
jokes.explanation: Selina's decision-making process on full display — a rapid-fire internal debate that lands on 'I don't know' as the default executive position. 'Let's just go with I don't know' as a presidential directive is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: Give him a handjob if you have to, just get it done, okay?
jokes.setup: Selina is telling Gary to go distract Leon West at any cost while she handles a crisis.
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating instruction culminates in 'give him a handjob if you have to' — the presidential instruction crossing into the completely inappropriate while Gary presumably takes it in stride.
jokes.explanation: Selina's escalating desperation reaching sexual instruction is darkly funny. The comedy is partly in Gary presumably accepting this directive without protest. The casualness of delivery is what elevates it above a simple shock joke.

jokes.text: Wow, two weeks detained. — That is hard. — I was in a hotel, so it wasn't that bad.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to empathize with Leon's detention experience.
jokes.punchline: Leon matter-of-factly corrects Gary's sympathy: 'I was in a hotel, so it wasn't that bad' — deflating the martyrdom narrative and suggesting his captivity was merely an inconvenient business trip.
jokes.explanation: Leon's deflation of his own detention is both funny (prison is a hotel!) and character-establishing (he's a pragmatic reporter, not a martyr). Works well as dark understatement.

jokes.text: Some people might think that the American government detaining an American journalist so that you can have a photo op might look bad. Can you see that?
jokes.setup: Selina tries to spin the situation to Leon.
jokes.punchline: Leon delivers the plain truth of what happened in the form of a rhetorical question — devastating in its specificity and the polite framing ('Can you see that?') of something catastrophic.
jokes.explanation: Leon's 'Can you see that?' — politely asking the President to acknowledge her own scandal — is darkly funny in its civility. The rhetorical structure mirrors political spin-speak back at its practitioners.

jokes.text: And when Ben and I were first discussing the peace talks, I said to Ben, I said, 'Ben. Ben, I...' — Ma'am, State Department. — This I've got to take. Excuse me.
jokes.setup: Selina is about to tell a story that will presumably incriminate her further with Leon.
jokes.punchline: She's rescued by a State Department phone call — visibly relieved to escape her own confession — and 'This I've got to take' is delivered as if it's the most important call of her life (because it is, as an escape route).
jokes.explanation: The timing of the call as a rescue device is well-executed. The comedy is in Selina's obvious relief and 'This I've got to take' as transparent escape — Leon's 'Never stops, does it?' as the unimpressed witness makes it land.

jokes.text: Okay, maybe the guys in the press plane would like me to go over there and tell them about the US government imprisoning a guy in Iran.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to persuade Leon to rest; Leon threatens to share his story with the press instead.
jokes.punchline: Leon's version of his story — 'the US government imprisoning a guy in Iran' — is maximally incriminating, and he delivers it as a casual threat while declining to rest.
jokes.explanation: The casual framing of the diplomatic scandal as 'a guy in Iran' is darkly funny. Leon's reporter instinct to just... go report it, against Gary's desperate containment efforts, is well-observed.

jokes.text: We've got to hold him hostage here. Do you understand? We've got to take off now. — Mike and Gary are off the plane. — We have a pilot on the plane, right? Okay, so let's go. We've got to go.
jokes.setup: Selina realizes Leon is about to go to the press plane and decides to take off immediately to trap him.
jokes.punchline: Selina describes her plan as 'holding him hostage' — echoing exactly what she did to him in Iran — and immediately confirms only a pilot is needed, willing to abandon her own staff.
jokes.explanation: The irony of Selina literally deciding to re-detain the man she just freed — and using the word 'hostage' — is sharp and dark. Her casual willingness to abandon Gary and Mike ('we have a pilot, right?') is another layer of characterization. Great.

jokes.text: Leon, you've got to sit down. — I'm a bad flier and I might need somebody to hold me. — Are you seriously detaining me again? — Am I being rendered? — No, you're being friendered. So just please accept our compulsory hospitality.
jokes.setup: Selina forces Leon to sit as Air Force One takes off, and Leon realizes he's being trapped again.
jokes.punchline: 'You're being friendered' — a portmanteau of 'rendered' (extraordinary rendition) and 'friended' — is followed by 'compulsory hospitality,' which precisely describes both what's happening and what 'friendly' American detention looks like.
jokes.explanation: One of the best lines in the episode. 'Friendered' is an extraordinary coinage — it captures the entire theme of the episode (American democratic detainment dressed up as hospitality), lands as a perfect pun, and 'compulsory hospitality' as the follow-up is equally brilliant. This is Veep writing at its peak. The joke does triple work: punchline, political satire, and thematic statement.

jokes.text: Oh, Jesus, that suit. My gosh, you look like a middle-school teacher.
jokes.setup: Dan arrives at Sidney's office, presumably in his standard work suit.
jokes.punchline: Sidney's fashion insult — 'middle-school teacher' — is a specifically emasculating comparison, reducing Dan's professional image to something powerless and underpaid.
jokes.explanation: 'Middle-school teacher' is a more interesting insult than 'you dress badly' — the specific power differential and social status implied is funny. Economical character work.

jokes.text: She's not exactly government-issue herself, is she? — Yeah. Hey, don't even look at her. — Oh. — I'm kidding. Or am I? — Huh? No, seriously, I'm just fucking with you, Dan.
jokes.setup: Dan comments appreciatively on Erica; Sidney immediately claims ownership/protection.
jokes.punchline: Sidney's 'I'm kidding. Or am I?' followed by 'No, seriously, I'm just fucking with you' creates an extended dominance game where Dan can never be sure what's a threat and what's a joke.
jokes.explanation: Good Sidney character work — establishing him as someone who uses ambiguity as power. The 'Or am I?' pause is effective cringe.

jokes.text: We've just started lobbying for glacé cherries, so mention glacé cherries. — Well, how do I work in glacé cherries into a political roundtable? — You'll figure it out. Listen, you give me glacé cherries, and I'll give you a bed of money with pussy on the nightstand.
jokes.setup: Sidney instructs Dan to mention glacé cherries on a political TV panel, which is obviously absurd.
jokes.punchline: Sidney's reward for the glacé cherry mention escalates into the completely unhinged — 'a bed of money with pussy on the nightstand' — which is both maximally crass and somehow entirely consistent with his established character.
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of lobbying for glacé cherries is already funny; the reward — a bed of money with pussy on the nightstand — escalates into surrealist territory that still feels consistent with Sidney's established vulgarity. The specificity of glacé cherries (such a fussy, specific product) is perfect.

jokes.text: Hey, that's Air Force One. — Oh, my God. What the hell is happening? — Why are they leaving us? — Are we at war? — Is it here? Sweet Jesus. — No, it can't be. It can't be. — Oh, sweet Jesus. — Stop saying Jesus. Stop it!
jokes.setup: Gary and Mike are outside when Air Force One suddenly starts to depart without them.
jokes.punchline: The panic escalates from 'why are they leaving us?' to 'are we at war?' in seconds, and Gary's repeated 'Sweet Jesus' responses are so unhelpful that Mike has to explicitly tell him to stop invoking Christ.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation panic sequence. 'Are we at war?' is a great leap in catastrophizing, and Mike telling Gary to stop saying Jesus is perfectly irritated. The comedy of being left behind in Iran compounds existing anxieties beautifully.

jokes.text: This is how I see it. I say the US is planning a secret trip. The Iranians ask you to confirm the secret trip. You say there's no secret trip because it's a secret. They think I'm James Bond and detain me.
jokes.setup: Leon explains his theory of why he was detained by Iran.
jokes.punchline: His elegant logic — denied trip → presumed spy → James Bond — is both accurate and devastating as a summary of diplomatic incompetence leading to journalism being mistaken for espionage.
jokes.explanation: Leon's explanation is pitch-perfect — it's logically airtight in a way that exposes the absurdity of diplomatic secrecy. 'They think I'm James Bond' as the inevitable conclusion of a chain of official denials is sharp and funny.

jokes.text: Journalism is storytelling. You tell your story about your bravery, your integrity and how we rescued you and gave you warm nuts.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to coach Leon on how to tell his detention story in a way that's flattering to her.
jokes.punchline: After 'bravery' and 'integrity,' the climactic detail she adds is 'warm nuts' — the airline snack served as the capstone of American magnanimity.
jokes.explanation: 'Warm nuts' as the culminating gesture of American heroism is perfectly bathetic. The structure — bravery, integrity... warm nuts — is excellent. Selina reducing her presidency to snack service is exactly her character.

jokes.text: The Iranians detained me because of you. That's my story. They detained me an extra day because of you. That's my other story. — Now, you want to be rescued or not, you ungrateful shit?
jokes.setup: Leon lays out the two damaging stories he's going to write about Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's response to being presented with two massive scandals is to call Leon 'an ungrateful shit' — as if gratitude were owed despite the acknowledged wrongdoing.
jokes.explanation: The outrageous audacity of 'you want to be rescued or not, you ungrateful shit?' is quintessential Selina — she acknowledges the wrongdoing implicitly ('rescued') while simultaneously demanding gratitude for it. The cognitive dissonance is the entire joke and it's excellently executed.

jokes.text: Jonah, either get Leon's mom to the air base or buy yourself a very convincing wig.
jokes.setup: Amy gives Jonah an ultimatum about fetching Leon's mother.
jokes.punchline: The alternative to success is apparently becoming a disguised fugitive ('a very convincing wig') — the jump from task failure to disguise is absurd and delightfully disproportionate.
jokes.explanation: Sharp ultimatum structure. 'Buy yourself a very convincing wig' as the consequence of failure is specifically absurd — not 'you're fired' but 'you need to disappear.' The 'very convincing' qualifier is doing extra work.

jokes.text: Maybe he's bitten off his tracking device.
jokes.setup: Kent is missing and should be at the VP office.
jokes.punchline: Amy's theory — that Kent has bitten off his own tracking device — treats him as a kind of feral or dangerous animal that's escaped his handler.
jokes.explanation: Funny image — Kent as a tagged animal who's removed his own tracking device is consistent with his robotic, data-driven character being applied in a zoological frame. Concise and specific.

jokes.text: Gary and Mike have been left behind in Iran. Wonderful. It's 'Black Hawk Down' with Laurel and Hardy.
jokes.setup: Ben is informed that Gary and Mike have been stranded in Iran when Air Force One left.
jokes.punchline: 'Black Hawk Down with Laurel and Hardy' — the mashup of military catastrophe with slapstick comedy duo — perfectly describes both the seriousness of the situation and the specific incompetence of the people involved.
jokes.explanation: 'Black Hawk Down with Laurel and Hardy' is one of the episode's best insult constructions — culturally specific, maximally compressed, and doing double characterization (the situation is serious AND the people are buffoons). Among the episode's most quotable lines.

jokes.text: Amy, if anyone asks for me, I've gone outside to scream into the night. — Okay. — Have one for me, too.
jokes.setup: Selina announces she's stepping away from everything to scream.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'Have one for me, too' — treating screaming into the night as a shared recreational activity to be taken in turns — is perfect Veep team camaraderie under extreme duress.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful exchange. 'I've gone outside to scream into the night' is already funny, but Amy's 'Have one for me, too' treats it as a service Selina is performing on their behalf — an absurd normalization of total breakdown. Both halves are strong.

jokes.text: The press are saying that the VP is unhappy with the president. — That doesn't make me happy. — Because of the secret polling? Or because he's heard that someone has hacked the data of dead children? Or a third reason which is even worse than those?
jokes.setup: Bill reports political bad news; Amy responds with a list of possible reasons.
jokes.punchline: Amy escalates through increasingly terrible reasons — polling, dead children's data hacking — and then adds 'or a third reason which is even worse than those,' which she doesn't specify, implying there's something even more horrifying she hasn't named.
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation structure. The three-option list works as a dark comedy of errors — each reason is worse than the last — and 'a third reason which is even worse than those' without naming it is both funny and ominous. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: I grew a small beard here.
jokes.setup: Kent arrives late and is questioned about where he's been.
jokes.punchline: Kent's explanation — 'I grew a small beard here' — as a geographical/temporal account of his absence is delivered deadpan, treating facial hair growth as an alibi.
jokes.explanation: Six words of perfect Kent. His deadpan non-explanation (he grew a beard here, that's it, that's all you're getting) is pitch-perfect for the character. The economy is near-perfect — maximum character revelation per word.

jokes.text: Bill, of my various walking paces, I selected moderate to fast.
jokes.setup: Kent has been criticized for being late and presumably slow.
jokes.punchline: Kent's defense — that he has 'various walking paces' and selected 'moderate to fast' — treats walking speed as a technical parameter he consciously manages, which is both robotic and specific.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most character-specific joke. Kent discussing his 'various walking paces' as if he's a machine with selectable speed settings — and 'moderate to fast' as a defensively high selection — is comedic genius. It's a perfect encapsulation of Kent's data-brained, expressionless personality in a single observation. Maximum character integration. Highly comparable to the 8.0-8.5 anchor range and above it.

jokes.text: Ooh, am I in the wrong place? — Is this QVC?
jokes.setup: Amy sees Dan doing a TV segment as a political commentator.
jokes.punchline: Amy's 'Is this QVC?' — comparing Dan's new pundit role to a shopping channel — suggests his political commentary is equally substance-free consumerism.
jokes.explanation: Sharp media criticism wrapped in a personal insult. QVC as a comparison for cable news punditry has genuine satirical bite. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: The man whose dream vacation is hunting the street kids of Rio on a human safari.
jokes.setup: Amy references Sidney Purcell to Dan with apparent disgust.
jokes.punchline: Her description of Sidney's dream vacation — hunting street children on a human safari — is an extreme dark joke that both characterizes Sidney's monstrous worldview and functions as a satirical jab at predatory lobbying.
jokes.explanation: 'Hunting street kids of Rio on a human safari' is gloriously dark and specific — not just 'he's a bad person' but a vivid, horrible image that becomes a character shorthand. It's the kind of line that defines a villain in one sentence.

jokes.text: Sorry you got fired. You were almost good at your job, which really made you stand out.
jokes.setup: Amy and Dan are doing a pre-show pleasantries exchange before the TV segment.
jokes.punchline: 'Almost good at your job' is the insult, and 'which really made you stand out' doubles down — implying almost-competence is exceptional in their field.
jokes.explanation: Layered insult with excellent structure. 'Almost good at your job' is the first blade, and 'which really made you stand out' is the twist — implying that mediocrity in their world is distinguished performance. Two levels of contempt in one sentence.

jokes.text: I've never had strong feelings for a plane, but right now I would cheat on Wendy with that thing.
jokes.setup: Mike sees the backup plane that will get him out of Iran.
jokes.punchline: Mike's relief at seeing the plane is expressed through genuine romantic/sexual feeling — he would betray his wife for an aircraft — and the specificity of 'Wendy' makes it funnier.
jokes.explanation: The Wendy callback makes this land harder — Mike was so excited about going to the cottage with Wendy, and now he'd trade her for a backup plane. The specificity of naming Wendy transforms a generic 'I love this plane' line into something character-specific and emotionally resonant (in a ridiculous way). Very quotable.

jokes.text: Jonah, I know something's happening at the VP's office. Oh. Okay, you do. — I need you to tell me what's going on. And, uh, do I have to tell you that right now? — Yes, you do. — I was thinking we could stop for a coffee.
jokes.setup: Kent demands information from Jonah in the car with Leon's mother present.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's stalling — 'do I have to tell you right now?' followed by 'I was thinking we could stop for a coffee' — is transparently avoidant and funny because he's being pressed by Kent Davison of all people.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's stalling with 'I was thinking we could stop for a coffee' is funny — a completely absurd social deflection in response to a direct political demand. Sets up the Teddy revelation well.

jokes.text: Uh, Teddy's been touching me. — Or tea. — Uh, just processing that.
jokes.setup: Jonah is finally pressured to reveal information about the VP's office.
jokes.punchline: Jonah confesses Teddy has been sexually touching him; Kent's response is 'Or tea' — completely unrelated, clearly still processing the coffee deflection — followed by 'Uh, just processing that' when he catches up.
jokes.explanation: Among the episode's best moments. 'Teddy's been touching me' is already a bomb drop, but Kent's 'Or tea' — his brain still on the coffee/tea beverage exchange — followed by 'Uh, just processing that' is structurally masterful. The delay between the revelation and Kent's brain catching up is perfectly timed. The 'Or tea' as an accidental deflection creates an enormous comedic beat that the silence around it amplifies.

jokes.text: Um, he... He... he cupped my testicles. — On another occasion, he patted or tapped on my testicles. — And then on another occasion, he held my testicles for a significantly long time.
jokes.setup: Jonah describes Teddy's sexual misconduct to Kent with legal-deposition precision.
jokes.punchline: The escalating clinical vocabulary — 'cupped,' 'patted or tapped,' 'held... for a significantly long time' — and the bureaucratic 'on another occasion' framing make a serious matter unbearably funny through pure procedural language.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe comedy. The humor comes entirely from Jonah's formal, bureaucratic language for something deeply inappropriate — 'patted or tapped' as an uncertain description, 'a significantly long time' as a legal qualifier. The three-beat structure mirrors formal complaint language perfectly. This is dark but the comedy is in the language, not in trivializing the act.

jokes.text: Guess what. I just got engaged. — Are you fucking kidding me? Catherine's there, too? — Hi, Jonah. Jason proposed and I said yes. — Well, shit, congratulations. — Marriage is good. — It's a fine institution.
jokes.setup: Jonah's testicle testimony is interrupted by Catherine announcing her engagement.
jokes.punchline: Kent's 'Marriage is good' / 'It's a fine institution' — the most robotic possible wedding congratulations — delivered while presumably still processing the testicle descriptions, is perfect character comedy.
jokes.explanation: The tonal whiplash — from Jonah describing testicle molestation to Catherine announcing engagement — is brilliant scene construction. Kent's 'Marriage is good. It's a fine institution.' as his emotional range for wedding news is perfect Kent — the bare minimum social response delivered with robotic confidence.

jokes.text: Don't tell my mom. I want to surprise her. — Jonah, is there anything else you want to tell us? — I don't know. Is there anybody else in the room? — Just myself and Bill again. — And I'm here taking notes. — Sue's there?! Fuck! — Okay, well, no. There's nothing else that I have to say.
jokes.setup: Jonah has just revealed Teddy's touching and is asked if there's more; he checks who's listening.
jokes.punchline: Sue reveals she's been taking notes, and Jonah immediately shuts down — his willingness to share secrets is directly proportional to his confidence about the audience size.
jokes.explanation: The escalating speakerphone discovery is a classic Veep cringe structure. 'Sue's there?! Fuck!' as the moment of total exposure is perfectly delivered — Sue taking notes amplifies Jonah's paranoia, and his instant shutdown is character-specific and funny.

jokes.text: Okay, we got nothing out of that but a funny story.
jokes.setup: After the entire Teddy testicle revelation and engagement announcement phone call ends.
jokes.punchline: Kent's summary — 'we got nothing out of that but a funny story' — treats sexual harassment testimony as an information-gathering mission that failed while acknowledging its entertainment value.
jokes.explanation: Perfect button on the sequence. Kent treating Jonah's sexual harassment account as a failed intelligence-gathering exercise — 'nothing but a funny story' — is both deeply in character and darkly, uncomfortably funny. 'A funny story' as his assessment of what was just described is exactly the kind of Kent line that becomes a fan favorite.

jokes.text: Plane's broken. We can't take off. They said it's gonna be a week until they get the part. — Jesus Christ, the Chinese can 3-D print a hundred houses in that time.
jokes.setup: The backup plane is broken and the part will take a week.
jokes.punchline: Gary's context for how long a week is — '3-D printing a hundred houses' — is a real technological reference that makes his complaint simultaneously contemporary and completely beside the point.
jokes.explanation: 'The Chinese can 3-D print a hundred houses in that time' is a funny non-sequitur that reveals Gary reads technology news in a paranoid, competitive way. Interesting character beat though not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: We need to get on the press plane. — The press hates us, especially you. — They can't stand you. — We have booze, Gary. — And they have a crippling dependency. — What, is this gonna work? — In a dry country, the man with all the booze holds the cards.
jokes.setup: Mike and Gary need to get on the press plane, but the press hates them.
jokes.punchline: Mike's plan — leveraging alcohol in a dry country against a press corps with a 'crippling dependency' — is both pragmatically genius and darkly funny as a strategy.
jokes.explanation: The press corps alcoholism running gag pays off here as a strategic asset. 'In a dry country, the man with all the booze holds the cards' is a genuinely good line — it's political strategy distilled to its most elemental and absurd form. The callback gives it extra weight.

jokes.text: I specifically promised Doyle that I would not do that, Kent. — I apologize. But it did throw up some very interesting results. — But I will run those by you at a less angry time.
jokes.setup: Selina confronts Kent about secretly polling Doyle against other VP candidates.
jokes.punchline: Kent apologizes but immediately notes the interesting results — unable to suppress his data enthusiasm — then offers to share them 'at a less angry time,' which is the most analytical possible response to a political betrayal.
jokes.explanation: 'I will run those by you at a less angry time' is perfect Kent — he's scheduling his betrayal's data presentation based on Selina's emotional calendar. The polling results as something to be shared when she's ready is both apologetic and completely tone-deaf.

jokes.text: Why in the name of pixelated fuck would you do that? My God! — And can you please use code when we're talking about this? Use the word, I don't know, cupcake instead of data and use happy instead of bereaved or something.
jokes.setup: Selina reacts to Kent and Bill's plan to implicate Doyle in the dead kids' data scandal.
jokes.punchline: After 'pixelated fuck' (a great profanity construction), Selina demands code words and invents them on the spot — 'cupcake' for data and 'happy' for bereaved — making the scheme sound maximally absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'Pixelated fuck' is a great profanity coinage for the tech-data context. And 'cupcake' for data / 'happy' for bereaved is funny because the euphemisms are so cheerfully inappropriate. 'Use happy instead of bereaved' applied to dead children's parents is darkly perfect.

jokes.text: He will be as involved as the rest of us in targeting happy parents after stealing cupcakes about their dead children.
jokes.setup: Ben explains the plan using Selina's new code words.
jokes.punchline: Using the code, what sounds like it might be wholesome ('happy parents,' 'cupcakes') is revealed as a grotesque cover for 'targeting bereaved parents after stealing data about their dead children' — the cheerful words making the scheme sound more nightmarish.
jokes.explanation: The code-word payoff is excellent — 'targeting happy parents after stealing cupcakes about their dead children' achieves a surreal quality where the euphemisms make the underlying horror MORE apparent, not less. This is how good satirical writing about political doublespeak works.

jokes.text: Fine. Inject him with the happy cupcake virus, all right? I hope he swells up and dies. — That's not code, by the way.
jokes.setup: Selina agrees to the plan using the code words she invented.
jokes.punchline: 'Happy cupcake virus' is already a great phrase, but 'I hope he swells up and dies' — followed by someone immediately clarifying that's not code — is a perfect dark button.
jokes.explanation: 'Happy cupcake virus' is a great comedic coinage, and 'I hope he swells up and dies — that's not code, by the way' is a perfect two-part punchline. The 'that's not code' clarification means someone is worried this might be interpreted as policy, which is itself dark and funny.

jokes.text: Ben, why don't I know what's going on here? — I don't know. — I'm supposed to have my finger on the button. But for all I know, it's been rewired and I'm just operating some sort of light in a closet somewhere.
jokes.setup: Selina laments being out of the loop on major decisions happening in her name.
jokes.punchline: The button metaphor — presidential nuclear authority — evolves into the image of Selina unknowingly operating a closet light, which perfectly captures her sense of displacement from power.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's thematically richest jokes. The 'button' metaphor starts in presidential authority and ends in domestic irrelevance — a closet light. The specificity of 'some sort of light in a closet somewhere' is perfect. It's simultaneously a political joke, a character revelation, and a metaphor for the entire show's premise.

jokes.text: I'm gonna treat him like my own brother, ahem, who I had murdered back in '86.
jokes.setup: Selina is asked what they'll do about Leon West.
jokes.punchline: Selina's reassurance — treating Leon like family — is immediately undercut by the revelation that she had her brother murdered, delivered with a polite 'ahem' as the only acknowledgment of the bombshell.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: the warm familial framing ('my own brother'), the 'ahem' as the world's most understated pivot, and 'who I had murdered back in '86' as a completely matter-of-fact dark revelation. The year '86 specificity adds authenticity to the absurdity. This is dark comedy at the highest level.

jokes.text: You could say that the president's Iran visit puts a delicious glacé cherry on the cake of Selina Meyer's tour.
jokes.setup: Dan is on the TV panel and has been instructed to mention glacé cherries for Sidney's lobbying client.
jokes.punchline: Dan manages to work 'delicious glacé cherry' into a genuinely plausible sentence about the Iran visit — it makes sense but is clearly forced, and the word 'delicious' is the tell.
jokes.explanation: The glacé cherry payoff is excellent. Dan threads the needle — 'a delicious glacé cherry on the cake' is technically a normal metaphor, but the conspicuous specificity of glacé (rather than maraschino or regular cherry) and 'delicious' as a loaded descriptor signals the product placement perfectly. It rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: Well, welcome back from the wasteland. Here's to us. To a job well done on your part. — You were great. — Yeah, I know. — You know, we could still be great. — We could? — Oh, yeah.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy have a post-show drink and Dan builds up to asking Amy something.
jokes.punchline: The setup ('we could still be great') reads as romantic and the 'Oh yeah' and 'uh-oh' exchange confirm Amy reads it that way too — creating the misdirect.
jokes.explanation: Sets up the misdirection payoff well. The romantic framing ('we could still be great') with Dan's smooth delivery earns the audience's assumption — necessary infrastructure for the next joke.

jokes.text: Am I that transparent? Really? — Dan, you kind of are. — Well, Amy, I would love it if you would give me... access to the White House. — Oh. — What? — You think I was gonna ask you something else? — No. — Okay.
jokes.setup: The romantic misdirection built up over several exchanges is resolved.
jokes.punchline: Dan's ask — 'access to the White House' — is professional, not romantic. Amy's 'Oh' and the awkward beat where they both pretend she wasn't expecting something else is the punchline — her embarrassment confirms she absolutely was.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured misdirection payoff. The episode has been building to this and the double-bluff — Dan IS just asking for a professional favor, and Amy's 'Oh' reveals she hoped otherwise — does real emotional and comedic work simultaneously. The awkward 'You think I was gonna ask you something else? — No. — Okay.' exchange is beautifully uncomfortable.

jokes.text: Amy, bad news. Ericsson told Doyle that the campaign used dead kids' data to target recently bereaved parents. — God, that is some elaborate self-sabotage right there. That is Cirque du Soleil suicide bombing.
jokes.setup: Amy receives news that the campaign's plan has backfired catastrophically.
jokes.punchline: 'Cirque du Soleil suicide bombing' — the image of acrobatic precision applied to self-destruction — is both maximally absurd and oddly apt for describing unnecessarily elaborate political own-goals.
jokes.explanation: 'Cirque du Soleil suicide bombing' is extraordinary — the combination of high-art acrobatics with terrorism for the concept of elaborate self-destruction is a genuinely original and vivid phrase. It's exactly the kind of line that defines Veep's writing at its best: absurdist, violent, precise, and immediately quotable. Among the episode's top three lines.

jokes.text: I feel like I'm on a life-support machine and they keep pulling the plug to charge their phones.
jokes.setup: Selina describes her emotional state amid all the crises.
jokes.punchline: The life-support machine metaphor is immediately grounded in a contemporary, banal detail — 'pulling the plug to charge their phones' — transforming existential despair into a comment on tech-dependent indifference.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most original line. The life-support/phone-charging image is perfect — it takes a universal modern irritation (people prioritizing charging over consideration) and applies it to the ultimate stakes (life and death). The specificity of phones being the reason for the plug-pulling is what elevates it from a simile to genuine comedic invention. Top-tier Selina Meyer.

jokes.text: We've got about 10 seconds to drink everything and then eat the bottles.
jokes.setup: Mike and Gary are apparently in possession of alcohol they need to conceal in a dry country.
jokes.punchline: 'Eat the bottles' — the most extreme possible method of concealing alcohol evidence — crosses into physical impossibility, which is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Eat the bottles' is a great escalation of panic — the logical sequence (hide the evidence) reaching into the physically impossible. Mike's commitment to the impossible solution is the comedy.

jokes.text: Did you rest? — I slept like a drugged log.
jokes.setup: Gary asks if Selina rested.
jokes.punchline: 'I slept like a drugged log' — a scrambled version of 'slept like a log,' modified to include 'drugged,' which both explains the quality of sleep and references the Ambien storyline.
jokes.explanation: 'Drugged log' is a great modification of the idiom — it adds precision (the drug explains the depth of sleep) while maintaining the familiar structure. The Ambien callback thread pays off. Good economy.

jokes.text: Did my eye just twitch? — No.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about an involuntary facial movement.
jokes.punchline: Ben says 'No' immediately — almost certainly a lie, and definitely delivered in a way that suggests it's a lie.
jokes.explanation: A very short beat that works on the lie alone. Economy is perfect but there's not much comedic architecture beyond 'Ben lies flatly.' Functional.

jokes.text: Hey, Catherine, about earlier. — The molesting. — What? — Hmm? — Wait, what? — Hey, look, there's your mom.
jokes.setup: Jonah approaches Catherine, presumably to apologize or explain something awkward from earlier.
jokes.punchline: Catherine's first assumption about what 'earlier' refers to is 'The molesting' — a word she immediately regrets; Jonah's confused response and his instant redirect to 'there's your mom' confirms neither of them want this conversation.
jokes.explanation: Exceptional cringe moment. Catherine's knee-jerk 'The molesting' — confirming Jonah's situation is public knowledge in some form — and Jonah's instant 'hey there's your mom' as a redirect is perfectly executed. The mutual horror of both characters wanting out of this conversation simultaneously is well-structured. One of the episode's strongest cringe beats.

jokes.text: Um, her specialty is common sense. — That's right. Good memory.
jokes.setup: Amy relays Karen Collins's self-described specialty to Jonah.
jokes.punchline: Karen confirms 'common sense' as her specialty with 'That's right. Good memory.' — as if claiming 'common sense' as a special skill requires validation, and as if Amy remembering something said minutes ago is an impressive feat.
jokes.explanation: 'Common sense' as a professional specialty sets up Karen as a different kind of political character. 'Good memory' for recalling what was said a minute ago is a subtle bit of obliviousness. Character introduction comedy — functional.

jokes.text: Sue, if anybody asks for me, I've gone outside to scream into the night. — Okay. — Have one for me, too.
jokes.setup: A different instance or echo of the screaming-into-the-night moment, with Sue also present.
jokes.punchline: Amy adds 'Have one for me, too' — treating Selina's breakdown screams as a shared service, confirming the institutional collapse is universal.
jokes.explanation: This appears to be the actual instance (or a second instance) of the screaming-into-the-night exchange. The 'Have one for me, too' from Amy is excellent — treating screaming as a communal release activity is the right absurdist twist.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna make it to the cottage, Wendy. — I'm in Iran. — Turns out you can't buy a boarding pass with tiny bottles of Jack Daniels.
jokes.setup: Mike is on the phone with his wife, explaining why he won't make it to the cottage.
jokes.punchline: The explanation — that tiny Jack Daniels bottles don't function as currency for boarding passes in Iran — is both a specific detail from his alcohol-bribery plan AND a callback to the Wendy/cottage excitement.
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback convergence. Mike not making it to the cottage (callback to his excitement), in Iran (the whole episode's stakes), because tiny Jack Daniels bottles failed as currency — the specificity of 'tiny bottles' and the absurdity of the transaction attempt is genuinely hilarious. One of the episode's most satisfying laugh lines.

jokes.text: I'm not gonna make it to the cottage, Wendy. I'm in Iran. / Turns out you can't buy a boarding pass with tiny bottles of Jack Daniels.
jokes.setup: Mike calls Wendy to explain he's still stranded in Iran and can't make the cottage weekend.
jokes.punchline: 'Turns out you can't buy a boarding pass with tiny bottles of Jack Daniels' — the specific currency (airplane minibar bottles), specific destination (boarding passes), and flat 'turns out' implies this was an actual attempted transaction.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Tiny bottles of Jack Daniels' as currency is specific and vivid — we see the airline minibar bottles being offered to an Iranian airport official. 'Turns out you can't' implies he actually tried this. The callback to Wendy and the cottage completes that subplot thread. 'Turns out' is the magic word — it sounds like a learned lesson from experience. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: We're engaged. — No, you're not. — Yes, we are. — But we are. — I'm 48. — Put your hand down.
jokes.setup: Catherine and Jason announce their engagement to Selina.
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate 'No, you're not' — refusing to accept the engagement as real — and her 'I'm 48' as the primary objection (not about them, about her becoming a mother-in-law) followed by 'Put your hand down' is an extraordinary parental response.
jokes.explanation: Selina's reaction to her daughter's engagement being 'I'm 48' — making it entirely about her own aging — is peak Selina. 'Put your hand down' (presumably Jason has his hand up as if to speak) is the perfect authoritarian button. All-time Selina mother moment.

jokes.text: I'm not snacking. I'm on my diet. — It's a celery stick. — All right, it's a cookie. — I've had dates every day. — You know dates are a dessert?
jokes.setup: Gary confronts Selina about her diet as they walk off the tarmac.
jokes.punchline: Selina's escalating dietary confessions — celery that's actually a cookie, dates she didn't know were dessert — reveals her self-deception about her diet in a closing character beat.
jokes.explanation: A nice closing character beat — Selina's inability to admit she's not on her diet mirrors her larger political self-deceptions throughout the episode. 'You know dates are a dessert?' is a good final line — Gary gently educating the President. Good episode button.

tmdb_overview: President Meyer travels to Iran, where an American reporter is being detained.
insight: Political chaos peaks as Selina juggles 90 jokes in Tehran's absurdist pressure cooker.
